comment_text.ud4.2:4.2: The variant micchādiṭṭhigatena is the more obvious reading. However, micchādiṭṭhihatā appears at AN 4.49 without variants. Commentary accepts hata in both places. Here it glosses sassatādimicchābhinivesadūsitena.
comment_text.ud4.2:5.1: Commentary: rakkhitacitto assa, literally “be one of guarded mind”.

root_text.ud4.2:1.2: ekaṁ samayaṁ bhagavā kusinārāyaṁ viharati upavattane mallānaṁ sālavane.
root_text.ud4.2:1.3: Tena kho pana samayena sambahulā bhikkhū bhagavato avidūre araññakuṭikāyaṁ viharanti uddhatā unnaḷā capalā mukharā vikiṇṇavācā muṭṭhassatino asampajānā asamāhitā vibbhantacittā pākatindriyā.
root_text.ud4.2:2.1: Addasā kho bhagavā te sambahule bhikkhū avidūre araññakuṭikāyaṁ viharante uddhate unnaḷe capale mukhare vikiṇṇavāce muṭṭhassatino asampajāne asamāhite vibbhantacitte pākatindriye.
root_text.ud4.2:3.1: Atha kho bhagavā etamatthaṁ viditvā tāyaṁ velāyaṁ imaṁ udānaṁ udānesi:

translation_text.ud4.2:1.2: At one time the Buddha was staying in the sal forest of the Mallas at Upavattana near Kusinārā.
translation_text.ud4.2:1.3: Now at that time several mendicants were staying not far from the Buddha in a wilderness hut. They were restless, insolent, fickle, scurrilous, loose-tongued, unmindful, lacking situational awareness and immersion, with straying minds and undisciplined faculties.
translation_text.ud4.2:2.1: The Buddha saw those mendicants staying nearby.
translation_text.ud4.2:3.1: Then, understanding this matter, on that occasion the Buddha expressed this heartfelt sentiment:
translation_text.ud4.2:5.1: That’s why you should guard the mind,
translation_text.ud4.2:5.3: and right view at the fore.
translation_text.ud4.2:5.5: a mendicant who has overcome <j>dullness and drowsiness
